questions.question_text: Which activity requires a Maryland real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: Listing property for sale for compensation
distractors: An owner selling their own property
An attorney handling a real estate closing
A government employee appraising public land
questions.explanation: Listing, selling, buying, exchanging, or leasing real property for compensation requires a license. Owners selling their own property, attorneys acting within scope, and government employees in official capacity are exempt.

questions.question_text: A retired teacher helps her neighbor sell a house and accepts a $2,000 thank-you payment. Does she need a license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, she performed licensed activity for compensation
distractors: No, helping a neighbor is always exempt
No, the payment was a gift not a commission
Only if she advertised her services publicly
questions.explanation: Any person who assists in selling real property for compensation must hold a license. The nature of the payment or the relationship to the seller does not create an exemption.

questions.question_text: An attorney drafts a purchase agreement and negotiates terms on behalf of a buyer as part of her law practice. Is she required to hold a real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: No, attorneys acting within their scope are exempt
distractors: Yes, any negotiation requires a license
Only if she receives a commission
questions.explanation: Maryland exempts attorneys performing real estate activities within the scope of their legal practice. This exemption recognizes that attorneys are already regulated by the state bar.

questions.question_text: A county assessor values properties for tax purposes as part of her government role. Must she hold a real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: No, government employees in official capacity are exempt
distractors: Yes, all property valuation requires a license
Only if she also holds an appraiser certification
Only if the properties are commercial
questions.explanation: Government employees performing real estate-related activities in their official capacity are exempt from Maryland licensing requirements. This applies to assessors, planners, and similar public roles.

questions.question_text: A property management company leases apartments and collects rent for compensation. An employee argues no license is needed because they only handle rentals. Is this correct?
questions.correct_answer: No, leasing for compensation requires a license
distractors: Yes, only sales transactions require licensing
Yes, rental activity is regulated separately
Only the company owner needs a license
questions.explanation: Maryland requires a license for leasing real property for compensation. The licensing requirement applies to rental and lease transactions, not just sales.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must a Maryland salesperson candidate complete?
questions.explanation: Maryland requires salesperson candidates to complete 60 hours of pre-license education from an MREC-approved provider. This includes a mandatory 3-hour ethics component.

questions.question_text: A broker candidate has completed the required education. How many total hours of pre-license coursework has she accumulated?
questions.explanation: Maryland requires 135 hours of pre-license education for a broker license. This is significantly more than the 60 hours required for a salesperson license, reflecting the greater responsibilities of brokers.

questions.question_text: A salesperson candidate completed her 60-hour course in January 2025. By when must she pass both exam portions?
questions.explanation: Candidates must pass both portions of the licensing exam within 1 year of completing their pre-license education. A course completed in January 2025 expires in January 2026.

questions.question_text: The salesperson pre-license curriculum must include a mandatory module on which subject?
questions.explanation: Maryland's 60-hour salesperson pre-license education must include a 3-hour ethics component. This ensures all new licensees understand their ethical obligations before entering practice.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the national portion of the exam but fails the state portion. Thirteen months later, she retakes and passes the state portion. Can she obtain a license?
questions.correct_answer: No, she must retake the course because it expired
distractors: Yes, passing both portions is sufficient
Yes, if she pays a late application fee
Only if the MREC grants a time extension
questions.explanation: Both exam portions must be passed within 1 year of course completion. At 13 months, the course has expired, and the candidate must complete the pre-license education again before retesting.

questions.question_text: What is the entry-level real estate license in Maryland?
questions.explanation: The salesperson license is the entry-level license in Maryland. Salespersons must work under the supervision of a licensed broker and cannot operate independently.

questions.question_text: Lisa holds a broker license but chooses to work under another broker's supervision. What is her license classification?
questions.explanation: An associate broker is a person who holds a broker license but works under the supervision of another broker. This allows broker-licensed individuals flexibility in their practice arrangement.

questions.question_text: A broker designates one of her licensees to manage daily operations at a second location. What role is this licensee filling?
questions.explanation: A branch office manager is a broker-designated licensee responsible for managing operations at a branch office location. This role ensures proper supervision at each office location.

questions.question_text: Which license type permits the holder to operate independently and supervise other licensees?
questions.explanation: Only the broker license permits independent operation and the authority to supervise salespersons and associate brokers. Salespersons and associate brokers must work under a broker.

questions.question_text: Tom is an associate broker who wants to open his own firm and hire agents. What must he do regarding his license?
questions.correct_answer: Operate as an independent broker at his own firm
distractors: Obtain a separate franchise license from the MREC
Convert his license to a salesperson designation
Complete an additional 60 hours of education
questions.explanation: Tom already meets every broker qualification — associate brokers satisfy the same education, experience, and exam requirements as brokers. To open his own firm he applies to the MREC to be issued a broker license in place of his associate broker license and can then operate independently; no additional education or examination is required.
